senses.definition: in a distance so short that may cause danger, problems, or annoyance

examples.x: The car following us on the dark, winding road was too close for comfort.
The political situation in the region became too near for comfort when tensions escalated.
Having an open flame near the gas leak was too close for comfort, and we quickly evacuated.
The dog's aggressive behavior towards the children was too close for comfort, and we separated them immediately.
The market's unpredictability made the stock investment feel too close for comfort, leading to anxiety among investors.

senses.definition: to put in great effort and take great risks to succeed or to fail trying

examples.x: With the championship on the line, the athlete knew it was do or die in the final race of the season.

senses.definition: to put everything at risk in order to achieve a particular goal

examples.x: The athlete knew it was the final round, so he decided to go for broke in pursuit of the gold medal.
Facing a seemingly unbeatable opponent, the underdog team decided to go for broke with an all-out offensive strategy.
In the face of adversity, she decided to go for broke in her career, taking on ambitious challenges to reach her dreams.
Despite the challenges, they went for broke and launched their own business.
The athlete knew it was the final round, so he went for broke in pursuit of the gold medal.

senses.definition: in a situation that involves a lot of risk and little support or evidence, especially one that arouses negative reactions in others

examples.x: Taking the controversial stance, the politician found herself out on a limb with few supporters.
The scientist ventured out on a limb by proposing a groundbreaking theory that challenged existing beliefs.

senses.definition: to do something that has the risk of death or serious injury

examples.x: He risked life and limb to save the drowning child from the swift river currents.
The firefighters sacrificed life and limb battling the raging forest fire to protect the community.
The astronaut was willing to risk life and limb to explore the uncharted territory of outer space.
The mountain climber faced extreme conditions and risked life and limb to reach the summit.
The humanitarian worker sacrificed life and limb to provide aid in war-torn regions, despite the perilous circumstances.

senses.definition: to say or do a thing that can put one in trouble or danger

examples.x: Despite the potential backlash, he decided to stick his neck out and speak up against the unjust policy.
The employee stuck his neck out by proposing a new, untested approach to the project.

word_list.pronunciation: wˈɔːk skˈeɪt tɹˈɛd ˌɑːn θˈɪn ˈaɪs

senses.definition: to be doing something that may get one into serious trouble or danger at any moment

examples.x: With the budget cuts looming, any additional spending could be like walking on thin ice.
I don't want to tread on thin ice with my boss by missing the project deadline.

word_list.pronunciation: tˈeɪk wˈʌnz lˈaɪf ˌɪntʊ wˈʌnz ˈoʊn ɔːɹ hˈændz

senses.definition: to do something so risky that a person may lose their life because of it

examples.x: When he decided to cross the treacherous river on a makeshift raft, he was truly taking his life into his own hands.
Choosing to travel alone through the dangerous wilderness, she was clearly taking her life into her own hands.
Venturing into the unknown, deep into the cave system, they were effectively taking their lives into their hands.
Running with the bulls in Pamplona is an example of people taking their lives into their hands for the thrill of the experience.
By attempting to negotiate a peace deal in the midst of the conflict, the diplomat was taking his life into his own hands, hoping for a peaceful resolution.

senses.definition: by accepting the responsibility for all potential dangers and risks involved

examples.x: Visitors are welcome to explore the abandoned factory, but do so at your own risk; the site is unsafe and unstable.
The company offers free Wi-Fi, but using it for sensitive transactions is at your own risk, as it may not be secure.
He decided to skydive without proper training, fully aware that it was at his own risk.
The sign near the cliff edge warned hikers that venturing too close to the edge was at their own risk due to the danger of falling rocks.
Leaving valuable items in your car at the parking lot is at your own risk, as the management does not guarantee security.

senses.definition: to add to the risks or demands of a competition, dispute, etc. that will lead to either more benefits or losses

examples.x: To win the contract, the company decided to up the ante by offering a lower price.
The negotiations were getting too comfortable, so he raised the ante with a counterproposal.
The team decided to up the ante by working overtime to meet the tight deadline.
In the final round of the competition, she raised the ante by performing a challenging routine.
To stand out in the job market, he knew he had to raise the ante by acquiring more skills and qualifications.

senses.definition: to sacrifice one's life so that someone or something can continue to exist

examples.x: The soldier was willing to lay down his life to protect his comrades on the battlefield.
She chose to lay down her life to save the drowning child in the turbulent river.
Martin Luther King Jr. inspired many with his commitment to civil rights and was ultimately willing to lay down his life for the cause.
The firefighter heroically laid down his life while trying to rescue a family trapped in a burning building.
In tales of heroism, characters often make the choice to lay down their lives to ensure the survival of their loved ones or their community.

senses.definition: to take great risks that could result in one's own death

examples.x: Crossing the busy highway without using the pedestrian crossing is like dicing with death.
Venturing into the wilderness without proper equipment or supplies is dicing with death.
He dices with death every time he jumps out of a plane.
They have diced with death in their past adventures.
I'm warning you not to dice with death by entering that abandoned building.

senses.definition: to push the limits of safety or good fortune by doing something that may result in harm or disaster
